coredata.dc:title: A nanoporous Three-dimensional graphene aerogel doped with a carbon quantum Dot-TiO2 composite that exhibits superior activity for the catalytic photodegradation of organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Shen
Wang, Hongbo
Fu, Jiajia
coredata.dc:description: Three-dimensional (3D) graphene aerogel (3DGA) is an emerging photocatalyst support that imparts improved charge-carrier mobility. In this study, a 3DGA framework doped with carbon-quantum-dot-decorated TiO2 nanosheets (3DGA@CDs-TNs) is fabricated by a hydrothermal method. 3DGA@CDs-TNs is then applied as a catalyst to degrade organic pollutants in aqueous solutions. The degradation rates for Cong-red (CR, 120 mg L-1), Rhodamine B (RhB, 20 mg L-1), and colorless tetracycline (TC, 100 mg L-1) reach 98.7% (after 2 h), 97.6% (after 2 h), and 99.8% (after 40 min), respectively, under visible-light irritation, and these rates are much higher than those for CDs-TNs and 3DGA alone. Meanwhile, the dyed wastewater was collected to verify the practicability of 3DGA@CDs-TNs. These enhanced efficiencies arise from the unique 3DGA@CDs-TNs configuration, in which the conductive 3DGA aerogel acts as an effective electron mediator that connects the CDs-TNs composite fragments, providing channels for fast charge transfer. In addition, 3DGA@CDs-TNs also exhibits excellent stability during recycling experiments, confirming its durability and practicability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Three-dimensional graphene aerogel (3DGA)
CDs-TNs
Organic pollutants
Photocatalysis
Recycling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of graphene oxide (GO), TNs and CDs-TNs nanocomposites
Dyeing of cotton fabric and acrylic fabric
Pathway and mechanism of photocatalytic activity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by National Key R&D Program of China (NO. 2019YFA0706900 ); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2019T120390 ); Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral research funds (No. 2018K018A ); and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (NO. JUSRP22041 ). The authors would also like to thank Shiyanjia lab for their professional tests support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD (a) and FTIR (b) patterns for GO, CDs-TNs, 3DGA, and 3DGA@CDs-TNs.
XPS spectra of GO, 3DGA, and 3DGA@ CDs-TNs. (a) Survey spectrum, (b) Ti2p, (c) O1s, and (d) C1s.
SEM (a–c) and TEM (d–f) images of GO, 3DGA, and 3DGA@CDs-TNs, respectively, and corresponding EDS mapping images for C, N, O, and Ti in 3DGA@CDs-TNs.
ESR spin-trapping profiles for (a) DMOP- ⋅ O2 − and (b) ⋅ OH generation over 3DGA@CDs-TNs.
Transient photocurrent densities for GO, 3DGA, and 3DGA@CDs-TNs under intermittent visible-light irradiation.
Photodegradation of (a) CR, (b) RhB, and (c) TC by the prepared materials under visible light; (d) Stability test results for CR degradation by 3DGA@CDs-TNs.

Pictures of cotton and acryl fabric before and after dyeing (a-b), Changes of the characteristic absorption after degradation by 3DGA@CDs-TNs under visible light irradiation (c) CR wastewater and photographs of residual CR solution (inset); (d) RhB wastewater and photographs of residual RhB solution (inset).
Proposed pathways for the photocatalytic degradation of (a) CR, (b) RhB, and (c) TC over 3DGA@CDs-TNs under visible-light illumination.
Dyeing parameters of CR for Cotton fabric.
Dyeing parameters of RhB for Acrylic fabric.
Comparison of CR, RhB and TC degradation over different photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: A nanoporous Three-dimensional graphene aerogel doped with a carbon quantum Dot-TiO2 composite that exhibits superior activity for the catalytic photodegradation of organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Centre for Functional Textiles, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Centre for Functional Textiles, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Centre for Functional Textiles, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-textiles, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-textiles, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-textiles, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Three-dimensional (3D) graphene aerogel (3DGA) is an emerging photocatalyst support that imparts improved charge-carrier mobility. In this study, a 3DGA framework doped with carbon-quantum-dot-decorated TiO2 nanosheets (3DGA@CDs-TNs) is fabricated by a hydrothermal method. 3DGA@CDs-TNs is then applied as a catalyst to degrade organic pollutants in aqueous solutions. The degradation rates for Cong-red (CR, 120 mg L-1), Rhodamine B (RhB, 20 mg L-1), and colorless tetracycline (TC, 100 mg L-1) reach 98.7% (after 2 h), 97.6% (after 2 h), and 99.8% (after 40 min), respectively, under visible-light irritation, and these rates are much higher than those for CDs-TNs and 3DGA alone. Meanwhile, the dyed wastewater was collected to verify the practicability of 3DGA@CDs-TNs. These enhanced efficiencies arise from the unique 3DGA@CDs-TNs configuration, in which the conductive 3DGA aerogel acts as an effective electron mediator that connects the CDs-TNs composite fragments, providing channels for fast charge transfer. In addition, 3DGA@CDs-TNs also exhibits excellent stability during recycling experiments, confirming its durability and practicability.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive depletion and abuse of aquatic resources has brought about environmental pollution, water contamination, and energy shortages.[1] Among the various types of water pollution, wastewater containing organic dyes is especially ruinous.[2] The uncontrolled release of organic dyes by the textile, paper, cosmetics, ink, and leather industries poses a serious threat to aquatic environments and human health.[3–5] Owing to their chemical stability, organic dyes can persist in water for extended periods. Therefore, green and low-cost technologies for water remediation and ameliorating environment risks are required.[6–9]

Organic compounds are currently degraded by photocatalysis, ozone degradation, Fenton oxidation, and electrochemical degradation.[10–13] Of these, photocatalysis has garnered substantial research attention because of its potential for wastewater purification.[14–17] Accordingly, the use of photocatalytic techniques that exploit oxide surface excitation under solar illumination as a means to decolorize organic dyes and reduce metal ions is a viable strategy for treating industrial effluents.[18–21] The photocatalytic ability of such catalysts comes from the generation of electrons in the conductive band and holes in the valence band (VB), which occurs upon exposure to sunlight. The electron-hole pairs migrate to the catalysts surface and produce highly active radicals (OH•, O•2 -), which are capable of destroying or mineralizing nearby toxic pollutants.[22,23] However, the poor cleaning efficiency and recyclability of powder catalysts has impeded their widespread application and popularization. It is therefore extremely important that these drawbacks are addressed by the development of multifunctional and robust supports with large surface areas, good recyclabilities, and high charge-carrier mobility.[24,25]

3DGA exhibits many of the numerous merits of graphene and has been demonstrated to be an excellent support material for photocatalytic applications.[26,27] Thus, incorporating a photocatalyst into a 3DGA provides a hybrid material that not only prevents catalyst particles from aggregating and/or dispersing in the liquid phase, it also provides enhanced pollutant adsorption.[28] Furthermore, the 3D framework of the 3DGA provides convenient charge-transfer routes and thus superior carrier transport that accelerates the delivery of photogenerated electrons.[29] Moreover, the photocatalyst can be easily deposited on the surface of the 3DGA owing to its unique functional properties, while its nanoporous structure provides highly efficient sunlight harvesting and intimate contact with contaminants.[30–32]

In the present study, carbon quantum dot (CD)-decorated TiO2 nanosheets (CDs-TNs) were hybridized with 3DGA (3DGA@CDs-TNs) by simple addition of CDs-TNs during the hydrothermal synthesis of 3DGA (Scheme 1 ). The resulting material was applied to the degradation of the organic contaminants CR, RhB, and colorless TC under visible light. Besides, the dyeing solution was collected to evaluate the practicability of photocatalysts (Table1 and Table2 , owf is the mass ratio of dye to fabric). More importantly, the degradation pathways for CR, RhB, and TC are proposed based on the identification of process fragments via liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Furthermore, a feasible visible-light photocatalytic mechanism for 3DGA@CDs-TNs is proposed.

Graphite powder (99.8%), anhydrous ethanol, tetrabutyl titanate, congo-red, and citric acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. Rhodamine B, sodium nitrate, sulfuric acid, potassium permanganate, hydrochloric acid, hydrofluoric acid, sodium hydroxide, sodium chloride, peregal O, acetic acid, sodium acetate and hydrogen peroxide were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Ammonium hydroxide was provided by J&K Scientific Ltd. Deionized water was supplied by a ULUPURE water system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene oxide (GO), TNs and CDs-TNs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: GO was synthesized by Hummers’ method.[33] TNs and CDs-TNs were synthesized using a thermal method according to our previous report. The detailed works are shown in the Supporting Information.

120 mg of prepared GO was added into 20 mL DI water under constant stirring to form a uniform solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, the resulting product was collected and washed with anhydrous ethanol and DI water, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h in an oven.

3DGA@CDs-TNs were prepared by a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 40 mg CDs-TNs was dispersed into 20 mL homogeneous GO solution (6 mg/mL) to achieve a CDs-TNs/GO mixture. Then, the solution was placed into 100-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h to prepare 3DGA@CDs-TNs. Subsequently, the formed 3DGA@CDs-TNs was washed with anhydrous ethanol and ID water, and dried by freeze-drying for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dyeing of cotton fabric and acrylic fabric
ce:section.ce:para: For CR dye, 1 g cotton fabric was dyed at a liquor-to-material ratio of 50: 1 with 0.02 g CR dye. The temperature of the dyebath was raised to 90 °C within 15 min, and kept for 15 min. Then 0.25 g NaCl was added to the solution with stirring for 15 min. After that, 0.25 g NaCl was dispersed into the above solution, and held for 15 min. Upon the dyeing was complete, the temperature was cooled down to 50 °C, and the dyed fabric was washed with fresh water. Finally, the dyed wastewater was collected for further use.

For RhB dye, 0.01 g sodium acetate, 0.025 g acetic acid, 0.005 g peregal O, 0.006 g RhB and were dispersed into 100 mL ID water to obtain dyebash. 1 g acrylic fabric was dyed at the above solution with the temperature of 85 °C for 40 min. Then, the temperature of dyebash was increased to 100 °C at the rate of 1 °C/min, and held for 30 min. After the temperature lowered to 50 °C, the dyed fabric was taken out and washed, the dyed wastewater was achieved.

The chemical states of catalysts were scanned by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS) under Cu Kα radiation in the range 10° to 80°. The microstructures and morphologies of the samples were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with a su1510 microscope and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with an JEM-2010 electron microscope (JEOL). EDX elemental mapping was used to identify the elemental compositions of the samples. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a NICOLET is10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was detected by a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI with a base vacuum operated at 300 W. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent response were measured by a Zennium PP211 electrochemical workstation.

Electrochemical measurements were tested in a CHI-760E workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China) using Ag/AgCl as the electrode, Pt wire as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The electrolyte solution was 0.5 M Na2SO4 (pH = 6.8). EIS measurements were conducted in the 0.5 M Na2SO4 (pH = 6.8) solution using a sinusoidal wave with an amplitude of 5 mV with a frequency of 100 kHz-0.1 Hz under open circuit potential conditions. Photocurrent measurements were carried out under visible-light simulation with 20-s on–off runs.

The degraded compounds and final fragments of CR, RhB, and TC were detected by LC-MS/MS (Waters, USA) using a BEH C18 column (1.7 μm, 2.1 × 150 mm). The mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile (as eluent A) and formic acid (0.1 M, as eluent B) at a flow rate of 0.3 mL min−1 employing a linear gradient of 0% B to 100% B over 15 min. Mass spectra were collected in positive ionization mode over the m/z = 20–2000. Experiment parameters were as follows: fragmentor 3.5 V, capillary voltage 20 V, desolvation gas flow 700 lit/h., source block temperature 100 °C, desolvation temperature 400 °C.

The photocatalytic abilities of the prepared samples were investigated by the decopmosition of CR (120 mg/L), RhB (20 mg/L), and TC (100 mg/L) under simulated visible-light supplied by a 300 W Xe lamp (λ ≥ 420 nm). Typically, 40 mg photocatalyst was added to 40 mL aqueous dye solution, and the solution was stirred in darkness for 30 min to obtain adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Every 30 min, 3 mL of the reaction solution was samples and then purified through centrifugation. The cleared liquid was measured using UV–vis spectrophotometry, monitoring the maximum absorption wavelength of CR at 498 nm, RhB at 554 nm, and TC at 357 nm.

Fig. 1 is the XRD spectra of the samples. The spectrum of GO shows a peak at 13.1° due to the (002) diffraction.[34] Comparing 3DGA to GO, 3DGA presents a broad diffraction peak at 20.1° that corresponds to the (002) diffraction, while the main peak for GO is disappeared, suggesting that GO is reduced and the oxygen containing functional groups are removed.[35] For the CDs-TNs composites, several distinct peaks are detected and the results are analyzed in Supporting Information. Moreover, for the 3DGA@CDs-TNs sample, the characteristic 3DGA peaks are shifted to higher angles and peaks related to the CDs-TNs composite are observed, evidencing the chemical interaction between the CDs-TNs and graphene.

FTIR characterization was employed to verify the interaction of the 3DGA@CDs-TNs aerogel. As shown in Fig. 2 , the absorption peaks for GO at 3419, 1630, 1385, and 1056 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of –OH, C = C, C-OH, and C-O, respectively.[36] For the CDs-TNs sample, the peaks at 3419, 1630, and 1385 cm−1 are attributed to the presence of N-H, C-H, and C-N = of the CDs. Additionally, the peaks at 1124 and 1056 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of C-O-C and C-O. As compared to GO, the spectrum of the 3DGA aerogel has a lower peak intensity at 3419 and 1726 cm−1, confirming that the number of oxygenated functional groups on the GO decreases during the synthesis of the aerogels.[37,38] Furthermore, the 3DGA@CDs-TNs aerogel shows all the characteristic peaks mentioned, implying that the CDs-TNs is successfully incorporated in the 3DGA.[39]

XPS was conducted to elucidate the compositions and electron states of the samples, and the results are displayed in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3(a) is the survey spectra, which verifies that all the samples contain mainly C, N, and O elements. Additionally, a peak for Ti is shown for 3DGA@CDs-TNs, confirming the presence of Ti in 3DGA@CDs-TNs. Fig. 3(b) shows that the Ti2p signal for 3DGA@CDs-TNs can be fitted to three peaks at 458.9 eV, 460.4 eV, and 464.6 eV, corresponding to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2.[40] According to Fig. 3(c), the O1s spectra are clearly modified upon the incorporation of CDs-TNs. For both GO and 3DGA, the O1s band are divided into two peaks at ~ 532. 5 eV and ~ 530.6 eV, which are related to C-OH and C = O.[41,42] For 3DGA@CDs-TNs, the peak positions shift toward the lower binding energies with a change in peak area. As expected, the new peak at 531.3 eV is attributed to the O-Ti-O signal. In the C1s spectra of the GO sample, three distinct peaks at 284.6, 286.5, and 287.9 eV are observed (Fig. 3(d)), which are separately corresponding to C = C/C-C, C-O, and C = O.[43,44] For the 3DGA sample, the C-O and C = O intensity decrease significantly and the relative peak positions shift, confirming that GO is effectively reduced to 3DGA.[45] For the 3DGA@CDs-TNs, the shift of the peak at 285.1 eV may be accredited to C-Ti-O groups on the surface of 3DGA@CDs-TNs. These results demonstrate the incorporation of CDs-TNs composite by 3DGA and confirm that CDs-TNs can be supported by the aerogel.

The microstructural features and morphologies of GO, 3DGA, and 3DGA@CDs-TNs are displayed in Fig. 4 . A two-dimensional structure and smooth surface are clearly visible for GO.[46] 3DGA shows a 3D macroporous structure comprising connected graphene layers (Fig. 4(b)). The large pores offer a platform for the storage of photocatalysis products.[47] The structures of 3DGA and 3DGA@CDs-TNs are similar as they are both synthesized by the hydrothermal reduction of GO (Fig. 4(c)). Fig. 4(d-f) shows the corresponding TEM images for GO, 3DGA, and 3DGA@CDs-TNs. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the nanostructure of GO exhibits a creased and expanded layer morphology, which is a unique trait of sheet-like GO.[48] Fig. 4(e) shows the TEM image of 3DGA, which exhibits a folding structure. Interestingly, CDs-TNs randomly embedded into 3DGA aerogel are observed in Fig. 4(e). Furthermore, the C, N, O, and Ti mappings further demonstrate that CDs-TNs are embedded in the 3DGA aerogel.

Fig. 5 is the optical absorption of the samples. 0.2CDs/TNs nanocomposites present an absorption edge at about 700 nm due to the modification of CDs. For 3DGA, an absorption from 200 to 800 nm is observed. Once introducing with CDs-TNs, 3DGA@CDs-TNs show a flat curve with high adsorption coefficient in UV and visible range, suggesting the introduction of CDs-TNs to 3DGA effectively improve the light absorption ability of CDs-TN. Due to the absorption performance of the two, large fraction of the abundant solar flux consists of UV–vis light and visible light can be sufficient utilized for the photocatalytic process.

To gain further insight into the specific active radicals generated during photocatalytic degradation over 3DGA@CDs-TNs, ESR spin-trapping measurements were performed. As displayed in Fig. 5(a,b), no obvious peaks are shown for the dark reaction. However, after visible-light exposure, signals for .O2– radicals are observed (Fig. 5(a)). Four characteristic signals for .OH radicals with a peak intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 are observed (Fig. 5(b)), elucidating that the .O2– and .OH radicals are really generated on the 3DGA@CDs-TNs systems under light irradiation.[49,50]

The transient photocurrent responses of GO, 3DGA, and 3DGA@CDs-TNs were determined under intermittent visible-light irradiation for several on–off cycles. The results are displayed in Fig. 6 . 3DGA shows a higher photocurrent than that of GO, which directly proves that the 3DGA support endows the catalyst with superior current intensity, thus increasing its photocatalytic efficiency. Compared with GO and 3DGA, 3DGA@CDs-TNs presents a higher reversible photocurrent response under intermittent visible-light simulation, which is about 6 times higher than that of 3DGA, implying that the doping of 3DGA with CDs-TNs is a viable strategy to improve the charge-transfer rate of electron-hole pairs. 3DGA not only provides support for CDs-TNs nanoparticles, but also prevents electron-hole pair recombination by increasing the electron charge transfer rate, which, in turn, accelerates the photodegradation of pollutants [51]. Moreover, the photocurrent response lifetime of 3DGA@CDs-TNs is clear from its consistently reproducible response to irradiation over several cycles.

The decomposition of CR, RhB, and TC were executed to assess the photocatalytic performances of the photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation (Fig. 7 (a-c)). Fig. 7(a) shows the CR degradation rates over TNs, GO, 3DGA, and 3DGA@CDs-TNs. CR is barely degraded in the presence of TNs and GO under visible-light illumination. While the RhB, and TC removal ratios of TNs and GO are less than 10%. For pure 3DGA, only 33.1% degradation efficiency is achieved, where the absorption process dominates CR removal. Notably particularly, 3DGA@CDs-TNs shows the highest photocatalytic efficiency for CR degradation, with 98.7% CR removed during 2 h irradiation. The degradation efficiency gradually increases with prolonging the reaction time from 0 min to 120 min. In addition, 3DGA@CDs-TNs also exhibits superior degradation efficiencies for RhB and TC within 2 h and 40 min, which are 97.6% and 99.8%, respectively.

The reusability of 3DGA@CDs-TNs was evaluated using CR as a representative pollutant. As shown in Fig. 7 (d), there is no significant attenuation of CR degradation after five repeat runs. All these results directly indicate the excellent stability and practicality of 3DGA@CDs-TNs for water purification.

In Table 3 , the photocatalytic performance of 3DGA@CDs-TNs is compared with other catalysts reported previously. Obviously, taking into account the different experimental parameters, 3DGA@CDs-TNs exhibits outstanding photodegradation activity among these catalysts. 3DGA as support for CDs-TNs nanoparticles increases the adsorption efficiency of pollutants, which is beneficial for accumulating pollutants, and hence accelerate the degradation efficiency of 3DGA@CDs-TNs. Moreover, the extremely light weight enables 3DGA@CDs-TNs to float on the surface of the reaction system, which allows it to absorb more sunlight.

The pictures of dyed fabrics are shown in Fig. 8 (a-b), after dyeing experiments, the dyed cotton and acrylic presented red in color. And the treated pH value of CR and RhB solution were about 6.7–7 and 3–4. Fig. 8 (c-d) shows the changes main characteristic absorptions of dyeing wastewater (CR and RhB) under visible light irradiation in existence of photocatalysts, about 90% of pollutants can be removed over the 3DGA@CDS-TNs within 40 min of visible light irradiation, directly manifesting the 3DGA@CDs-TNs exhibits excellent practicability for dyeing wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pathway and mechanism of photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Although the target pollutants can be decomposed completely by 3DGA@CDs-TNs, compounds produced during the oxidation process may be underlying organic pollutants. Hence, the reaction intermediates for the degradation of the three pollutants and the final products were identified by LC-MS/MS (Table S1-S3). From these results, tentative degradation pathways for CR, RhB, and TC are proposed. As presented in Fig. 9
(a), the N = N bond in the CR substrate is likely attacked by active radicals, resulting in the formation of 1,2-amino-4-naphthalenesulfonic acid (m/z = 239.01), benzidine (m/z = 184.24), and an intermediate (m/z = 451.67). In addition, products with m/z = 208.23 (naphthalenesulfonic acid) and m/z = 174.96 are proposed as further oxidation fragments of 1,2-amino-4-naphthalenesulfonic acid (m/z = 239.01). Signals with m/z = 109.13 (2-aminophenol, 3-aminophenol, and/or 4-aminophenol), m/z = 94.11 (phenol), and m/z = 93.14 (aniline) are assumed as the breakdown products of benzidine (m/z = 184.24). Subsequently, ring-opening and mineralization reactions appear to generate products with m/z = 116.07, 118.09, 104.06, and 90.03 as well as 60.06, which are eventually transformed into CO2, H2O, and other molecules.

The plausible degradation products and pathways for RhB and TC were also deduced (Fig. 9(b,c)). The corresponding molecular structures and relative parameters for RhB and TC are presented in Table S2 and S3.
In summary, the two pollutants are ultimately converted via intermediates into CO2, H2O, and other eco-friendly molecules.

From all the experimental results, a feasible photocatalytic mechanism for 3DGA@CDs-TNs is proposed. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , when the 3DGA@CDs-TNs is excited with visible light, CDs are able to absorb this light (550–650 nm) and convert it into UV light, which causes the TNs to produce electrons-hole pairs. Then, the electrons migrate easily to the 3DGA, being further transported to the CDs surface to consume O2 and produce •O2 –. The holes remaining in the VB of the TNs ultimately oxidize H2O or OH– to •OH, leading to pollutant degradation. During the degradation process, 3DGA acts as both an electron conductor and donator, prolonging the lifetime of charge carriers and retarding the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby improving degradation efficiency.

3DGA@CDs-TNs was successfully fabricated by the hydrothermal reduction of GO with the addition of CDs-TNs. CDs-TNs nanocomposites contact with 3DGA through tight disorderly links, leading to high incorporation and excellent distribution. Compared to 3DGA and CDs-TNs alone, 3DGA@CDs-TNs exhibits remarkable photocatalytic performance for pollutant degradation under visible-light stimulation. This is because 3DGA acts as a highway that provides extended pathways for electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, 3DGA@CDs-TNs has a 3D network with a high surface area that provides more active sites for the decomposition of pollutants. In particular, the catalysts anchored on 3DGA exhibit expedited electron-hole migration and separation, thus accelerating photocatalytic degradation. Thus, 3DGA provides an excellent support for the encapsulation of CDs-TNs, allowing facile reclamation of the photocatalyst from liquid systems.
Shen Shen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Hongbo Wang: Writing – review & editing. Jiajia Fu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Key R&D Program of China(NO.2019YFA0706900); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No.2019T120390); Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral research funds(No. 2018K018A); and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities(NO. JUSRP22041). The authors would also like to thank Shiyanjia lab for their professional tests support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2021.151116.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-Ion Batteries. In Situ Growth of CoP3/Carbon Polyhedron/CoO/NF Nanoarrays as Binder-Free Anode for Lithium-Ion Batteries with Enhanced Specific Capacity (Small 11/2020)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Du, W. Ma, H. Li, Lithium‐Ion Batteries: In Situ Growth of CoP3/Carbon Polyhedron/CoO/NF Nanoarrays as Binder-Free Anode for Lithium-Ion Batteries with Enhanced Specific Capacity (Small 11/2020), Small, 16 (2020) 2070059.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-situ Fe-doped g-C3N4 heterogeneous catalyst via photocatalysis-Fenton reaction with enriched photocatalytic performance for removal of complex wastewater

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Co-Pd/BiVO4: High-performance photocatalysts for the degradation of phenol under visible light irradiation
